items.title: How the Glorious Revolution crushed the NIMBYs
items.author: Kara Dimitruk & Ben Southwood
items.blurb: It argues that England's 1688 revolution let Parliament dismantle fragmented property rights and rigid inheritance rules, clearing the way for the investment that drove industrialization.

items.title: How to fix transit construction in America
items.author: Matthew Yglesias et al.
items.blurb: A playbook of concrete fixes to planning, procurement, permitting, and state capacity aimed at closing the gap between America's high transit spending and its poor cost-effectiveness.

items.title: Congress cuts a deal on the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
items.author: Halina Bennet
items.blurb: Lawmakers reach a bicameral agreement on housing legislation even as housing starts decline and homeownership slips in parts of the country.

items.title: AI-Native Firms
items.author: Tyler Cowen
items.blurb: Research finds that startups built around AI employ fewer workers but skew toward engineers and flatter hierarchies while reaching valuations comparable to non-AI rivals.

items.title: Why the Human Genome's Tangled Physicality May Confound AI
items.author: Philip Ball
items.blurb: The genome behaves as a dynamic, context-dependent regulatory system rather than a linear blueprint, making it hard for AI to predict biology from sequence alone.

items.title: Science Is Political—and Spiritual
items.author: Nick Hilden
items.blurb: Physicist Chanda Prescod-Weinstein argues that scientific institutions are inherently political and spiritual endeavors that should center marginalized voices and resist authoritarian interference.

items.title: The Shingles Vaccine Reduces Dementia
items.author: Alex Tabarrok
items.blurb: Three recent studies suggest the Shingrix vaccine cuts dementia risk by roughly 20 percent, possibly by blocking the varicella zoster virus.

items.title: The wonder of epidurals
items.author: Phoebe Arslanagic-Little
items.blurb: Epidural anesthesia safely removes most childbirth pain while keeping mothers alert, yet access remains unevenly distributed between wealthy and developing nations.

items.title: A Cohort Perspective on Latin America's Fertility Transition
items.author: Tyler Cowen
items.blurb: New research points to women's education and shifts in economic sectors, rather than children's schooling outcomes, as the main drivers of Latin America's fertility decline.

items.title: Seven Perfect Shuffles Randomize a Deck of Cards. But How Many Sloppy Ones?
The Inventor of the Thinking Machine Didn't Worry. Neither Should You
Merit vs. Tenure: Reforming Federal Firing
In the Midst of Tornado Season, a Surprisingly Short History of Predicting Twisters
